Hensel-lifted i mod p^4 + mixed-order unit ratio; compute v_p(x^t-y^t)+v_q(x-y)+v_2(C(2t,t)) mod 1000

Let $p=41$ and $q=17$. Let $w$ be the unique integer with $0<w<41^{4}$ such that
$$
w\equiv 9 \pmod{41}\quad\text{and}\quad w^{2}\equiv -1 \pmod{41^{4}}.
$$

Define
$$
x:=41^{5}\bigl(w\cdot 17^{11}+41\cdot 17^{7}\bigr),\qquad y:=41^{5}\cdot 17^{11}.
$$

Let $a:=v_{41}(x)=v_{41}(y)$, and write $x=41^{a}u$, $y=41^{a}v$ with $41\nmid uv$.
Let $r\in(\mathbb{Z}/41^{4}\mathbb{Z})^{\times}$ be determined by
$$
r\equiv u\,v^{-1}\pmod{41^{4}},
$$
and let $t:=\operatorname{ord}_{41^{4}}(r)$.

Define
$$
N:=v_{41}(x^{t}-y^{t})\;+\;v_{17}(x-y)\;+\;v_{2}\!\binom{2t}{t}.
$$

Determine the unique integer $N_{0}\in\{0,1,\dots,999\}$ such that $N_{0}\equiv N\pmod{1000}$.

Solution.
Step 1 (valuation_vp_definition): Compute the basic valuations of $x$ and $y$.
Since $y=41^{5}\cdot 17^{11}$, we have $v_{41}(y)=5$ and $v_{17}(y)=11$.

Also
$$x=41^{5}\bigl(w\cdot 17^{11}+41\cdot 17^{7}\bigr)
=41^{5}\cdot 17^{7}\bigl(w\cdot 17^{4}+41\bigr).$$
Modulo $17$, the inner factor satisfies $w\cdot 17^{4}+41\equiv 41\not\equiv 0\pmod{17}$, so $v_{17}(x)=7$.

Modulo $41$, we have $w\cdot 17^{11}+41\cdot 17^{7}\equiv w\cdot 17^{11}\pmod{41}$.
Because $w\equiv 9\not\equiv 0\pmod{41}$ and $41\nmid 17$, it follows that $41\nmid (w\cdot 17^{11})$, hence $v_{41}(x)=5$.

Therefore $a=v_{41}(x)=v_{41}(y)=5$.

Step 2 (mod_check_root): Verify $9^{2}\equiv -1\pmod{41}$.
Indeed $9^{2}=81\equiv 81-41=40\equiv -1\pmod{41}$.

Lemma 1 (Euler criterion for $-1$): For an odd prime $p$,
$$
\left(\frac{-1}{p}\right)=(-1)^{(p-1)/2},
$$
so $-1$ is a quadratic residue modulo $p$ iff $p\equiv 1\pmod{4}$.
Justification: This is Euler's criterion applied to $-1$.

Step 3 (hensel_lifting_simple_root): Existence and uniqueness of $w$.
Let $f(X)=X^{2}+1$. From Step 2, $f(9)\equiv 0\pmod{41}$.
Also $f'(X)=2X$, so $f'(9)=18\not\equiv 0\pmod{41}$.

Lemma 2 (Hensel lemma, simple root): Let $p$ be prime and $f\in\mathbb{Z}[X]$.
If $f(a)\equiv 0\pmod{p}$ and $f'(a)\not\equiv 0\pmod{p}$, then for every $n\ge 1$ there exists a unique $A\pmod{p^{n}}$ such that $A\equiv a\pmod{p}$ and $f(A)\equiv 0\pmod{p^{n}}$.
Justification: The simple-root condition permits a unique correction step at each power of $p$.

Apply Lemma 2 with $p=41$, $a=9$, $f(X)=X^{2}+1$, and $n=4$. This gives a unique class modulo $41^{4}$ congruent to $9$ modulo $41$ satisfying $X^{2}\equiv -1\pmod{41^{4}}$. The interval condition $0<w<41^{4}$ selects the unique integer representative $w$.

Step 4 (multiplicative_order_mod_prime_power): Compute $\operatorname{ord}_{41^{4}}(w)$.
From $w^{2}\equiv -1\pmod{41^{4}}$ we get $w^{4}\equiv 1\pmod{41^{4}}$.
Also $w^{2}\not\equiv 1\pmod{41^{4}}$ because $-1\not\equiv 1\pmod{41^{4}}$.
Hence $\operatorname{ord}_{41^{4}}(w)=4$.

Step 5 (unit_factorization_x_equals_p_power_times_unit): Identify $u$ and $v$.
With $a=5$ set
$$
u:=w\cdot 17^{11}+41\cdot 17^{7},\qquad v:=17^{11}.
$$
Then $x=41^{5}u$ and $y=41^{5}v$.
From Step 1, $41\nmid u$ and $41\nmid v$, hence $41\nmid uv$.

Step 6 (modular_inverse_in_unit_group): Compute $r$ modulo $41^{4}$.
Because $\gcd(17,41)=1$, $v=17^{11}$ is invertible modulo $41^{4}$, so $v^{-1}\pmod{41^{4}}$ exists.
Then
$$
r\equiv u\,v^{-1}\equiv (w\cdot 17^{11}+41\cdot 17^{7})\cdot (17^{11})^{-1}\equiv w+41\cdot 17^{-4}\pmod{41^{4}},
$$
so in particular $r\equiv w\pmod{41}$.

Step 7 (teichmueller_principal_unit_split): Split off the $4$-torsion part.
Define
$$
s\equiv r\,w^{-1}\pmod{41^{4}}.
$$
Using Step 6,
$$
s\equiv (w+41\cdot 17^{-4})w^{-1}\equiv 1+41c\pmod{41^{4}},\quad c\equiv 17^{-4}w^{-1}\pmod{41^{4}}.
$$
Since $17^{-4}$ and $w^{-1}$ are units modulo $41$, we have $c\not\equiv 0\pmod{41}$, i.e. $41\nmid c$.

Lemma 3 (Order of a depth-$1$ principal unit modulo $p^{4}$): Let $p$ be an odd prime and let $c$ be an integer with $p\nmid c$.
In $(\mathbb{Z}/p^{4}\mathbb{Z})^{\times}$, the element $1+pc$ has exact order $p^{3}$.
Proof:
(i) Binomial expansion gives
$$
(1+pc)^{p^{2}}=1+\binom{p^{2}}{1}pc+\sum_{j\ge 2}\binom{p^{2}}{j}(pc)^{j}\equiv 1+p^{3}c\pmod{p^{4}},
$$
because for $j=2$ one has $v_{p}\!\left(\binom{p^{2}}{2}\right)=2$ and $(pc)^{2}$ contributes $p^{2}$, so the term is divisible by $p^{4}$, and all $j\ge 3$ terms are divisible by at least $p^{5}$.
Since $p\nmid c$, we have $1+p^{3}c\not\equiv 1\pmod{p^{4}}$, so the order does not divide $p^{2}$.
(ii) Now $(1+p^{3}c)^{p}\equiv 1+\binom{p}{1}p^{3}c\equiv 1+p^{4}c\equiv 1\pmod{p^{4}}$, so the order divides $p^{3}$.
Therefore the order is exactly $p^{3}$.

Apply Lemma 3 with $p=41$ to $s\equiv 1+41c\pmod{41^{4}}$, $41\nmid c$. This gives $\operatorname{ord}_{41^{4}}(s)=41^{3}$.

Step 8 (coprime_order_lcm): Compute $t=\operatorname{ord}_{41^{4}}(r)$.
In $(\mathbb{Z}/41^{4}\mathbb{Z})^{\times}$ we have $r\equiv ws$ and
$$
\operatorname{ord}_{41^{4}}(w)=4,\qquad \operatorname{ord}_{41^{4}}(s)=41^{3},\qquad \gcd(4,41^{3})=1.
$$

Lemma 4 (Coprime-order product rule): In an abelian group, if $\operatorname{ord}(a)=m$ and $\operatorname{ord}(b)=n$ with $\gcd(m,n)=1$, then $\operatorname{ord}(ab)=mn$.
Justification: If $(ab)^{k}=1$, then $a^{k}=b^{-k}$; raising to the $m$th power gives $b^{km}=1$, so $n\mid k$, and similarly $m\mid k$, hence $mn\mid k$.

Thus $t=\operatorname{ord}_{41^{4}}(r)=4\cdot 41^{3}$.
Since $41^{3}=68921$, we have $t=4\cdot 68921=275684$.

Step 9 (valuation_of_principal_unit_power): Compute $v_{41}(s^{4}-1)$.
Using $s\equiv 1+41c$ with $41\nmid c$,
$$
s^{4}-1=(1+41c)^{4}-1=4\cdot 41c+6(41c)^{2}+4(41c)^{3}+(41c)^{4}.
$$
All terms except $4\cdot 41c$ are divisible by $41^{2}$, and $41\nmid (4c)$, so $v_{41}(s^{4}-1)=1$.

Lemma 5 (valuation dominance): If $A\equiv B\pmod{p^{n}}$ and $v_{p}(B)<n$, then $v_{p}(A)=v_{p}(B)$.
Justification: Write $A=B+p^{n}M$; then $v_{p}(p^{n}M)\ge n>v_{p}(B)$, so the lower valuation term controls the valuation of the sum.

Step 10 (transfer_to_r4_minus_1): Compute $v_{41}(r^{4}-1)$.
Since $r\equiv ws$ and $w^{4}\equiv 1\pmod{41^{4}}$ (Step 4),
$$
r^{4}=(ws)^{4}\equiv w^{4}s^{4}\equiv s^{4}\pmod{41^{4}},
$$
so $r^{4}-1\equiv s^{4}-1\pmod{41^{4}}$.
Because $v_{41}(s^{4}-1)=1<4$, Lemma 5 yields $v_{41}(r^{4}-1)=1$.

Step 11 (compare_vp_on_both_sides): Transfer to $u^{4}-v^{4}$.
From $r\equiv u\,v^{-1}\pmod{41^{4}}$ we get $r^{4}\equiv u^{4}v^{-4}\pmod{41^{4}}$, hence
$$
u^{4}-v^{4}\equiv (r^{4}-1)v^{4}\pmod{41^{4}}.
$$
Thus for some integer $M$,
$$
u^{4}-v^{4}=(r^{4}-1)v^{4}+41^{4}M.
$$
Since $41\nmid v$, we have $v_{41}((r^{4}-1)v^{4})=v_{41}(r^{4}-1)=1$, while $v_{41}(41^{4}M)\ge 4$.
Therefore $v_{41}(u^{4}-v^{4})=1$.

Lemma 6 (LTE, difference form): If $p$ is an odd prime, $p\mid(A-B)$, and $p\nmid AB$, then for any integer $n\ge 1$,
$$
v_{p}(A^{n}-B^{n})=v_{p}(A-B)+v_{p}(n).
$$
Justification: Standard lifting-the-exponent lemma.

Step 12 (LTE_minus): Compute $v_{41}(u^{t}-v^{t})$.
Apply Lemma 6 with $p=41$, $A=u^{4}$, $B=v^{4}$, and $n=41^{3}$.
From Step 11, $v_{41}(A-B)=v_{41}(u^{4}-v^{4})=1$.
Also $41\nmid uv$ (Step 5), so $41\nmid AB$.
Therefore
$$
v_{41}\bigl((u^{4})^{41^{3}}-(v^{4})^{41^{3}}\bigr)=1+v_{41}(41^{3})=1+3=4.
$$
Since $(u^{4})^{41^{3}}=u^{4\cdot 41^{3}}=u^{t}$ and similarly for $v$, we have $v_{41}(u^{t}-v^{t})=4$.

Step 13 (vp_product): Compute $v_{41}(x^{t}-y^{t})$.
Because $x=41^{a}u$ and $y=41^{a}v$ with $a=5$,
$$
x^{t}-y^{t}=(41^{a}u)^{t}-(41^{a}v)^{t}=41^{at}(u^{t}-v^{t}),
$$
so
$$
v_{41}(x^{t}-y^{t})=at+v_{41}(u^{t}-v^{t})=5\cdot 275684+4=1378424.
$$

Lemma 7 (unequal-valuation min rule): If $v_{\ell}(X)\ne v_{\ell}(Y)$, then $v_{\ell}(X-Y)=\min\{v_{\ell}(X),v_{\ell}(Y)\}$.
Justification: If $v_{\ell}(X)<v_{\ell}(Y)$ then $X-Y=X+(-Y)$ is a sum of terms with unequal $\ell$-adic valuations, so the lower valuation term controls the valuation.

Step 14 (vp_sum_exact_when_unequal_at_q): Compute $v_{17}(x-y)$.
From Step 1, $v_{17}(x)=7$ and $v_{17}(y)=11$ are unequal, hence by Lemma 7,
$$
v_{17}(x-y)=\min\{7,11\}=7.
$$

Lemma 8 (central binomial $2$-adic valuation): For $n\ge 1$,
$$
v_{2}\binom{2n}{n}=s_{2}(n),
$$
where $s_{2}(n)$ is the number of $1$'s in the binary expansion of $n$.
Proof: Legendre's formula gives $v_{2}(m!)=m-s_{2}(m)$, so
$$
v_{2}\binom{2n}{n}=v_{2}((2n)!)-2v_{2}(n!)=(2n-s_{2}(2n))-2(n-s_{2}(n))=2s_{2}(n)-s_{2}(2n).
$$
Since $2n$ is a left shift of $n$ in base $2$, $s_{2}(2n)=s_{2}(n)$, hence $v_{2}\binom{2n}{n}=s_{2}(n)$.

Step 15 (binomial_2adic_via_digit_sum): Compute $v_{2}\binom{2t}{t}$.
Write
$$275684=262144+8192+4096+1024+128+64+32+4
=2^{18}+2^{13}+2^{12}+2^{10}+2^{7}+2^{6}+2^{5}+2^{2}.$$
This has $8$ binary $1$'s, so $s_{2}(t)=8$ and $v_{2}\binom{2t}{t}=8$.

Step 16 (assemble_N + mod_m_normalization): Assemble $N$ and reduce modulo $1000$.
We have
$$
N=v_{41}(x^{t}-y^{t})+v_{17}(x-y)+v_{2}\binom{2t}{t}=1378424+7+8=1378439.
$$
Therefore $N\equiv 439\pmod{1000}$, so the unique $N_{0}\in\{0,1,\dots,999\}$ with $N_{0}\equiv N\pmod{1000}$ is $N_{0}=439$.

Answer: $439$.

3. Uniqueness Test
1) The conditions $w\equiv 9\pmod{41}$ and $w^{2}\equiv -1\pmod{41^{4}}$ determine $w$ uniquely modulo $41^{4}$ by Lemma 2 (simple-root Hensel lifting). The restriction $0<w<41^{4}$ then fixes a unique integer $w$.

2) With $w$ fixed, $x$ and $y$ are explicit, hence $a=v_{41}(x)=v_{41}(y)$ and the $41$-units $u,v$ in $x=41^{a}u$, $y=41^{a}v$ are uniquely determined.

3) The class $r\equiv u v^{-1}\pmod{41^{4}}$ is uniquely determined because $v$ is invertible modulo $41^{4}$.

4) The decomposition invariant $s\equiv r w^{-1}\pmod{41^{4}}$ is uniquely determined, and Step 7 forces $s\equiv 1+41c\pmod{41^{4}}$ with $41\nmid c$. Lemma 3 then forces $\operatorname{ord}_{41^{4}}(s)=41^{3}$ uniquely. Together with $\operatorname{ord}_{41^{4}}(w)=4$ and $\gcd(4,41^{3})=1$, Lemma 4 forces $t=\operatorname{ord}_{41^{4}}(r)=4\cdot 41^{3}$ uniquely.

5) With this unique $t$, Steps 9-13 force $v_{41}(x^{t}-y^{t})$, Step 14 forces $v_{17}(x-y)$, and Step 15 forces $v_{2}\binom{2t}{t}$. Hence $N$ is uniquely determined.

6) The normalization rule "choose the unique $N_{0}\in\{0,\dots,999\}$ with $N_{0}\equiv N\pmod{1000}$" makes the reported answer unique, namely $439$.

439
